chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
locked_facts: Angular momentum L = Iω (for rotation about fixed axis)
τ = dL/dt — torque equals rate of change of angular momentum
If net external torque is zero, angular momentum is conserved: L = Iω = constant
When I decreases, ω must increase (and vice versa) to keep L constant
For a particle: L = r × p = mvr sinθ
Ice skater pulling arms in: I decreases → ω increases (spins faster)
Angular momentum is a vector quantity — direction along axis of rotation (right-hand rule)
truth_statements: Angular momentum is conserved when the net external torque on a system is zero
A spinning ice skater speeds up when they pull their arms in because moment of inertia decreases while angular momentum stays constant
The angular momentum of a particle about a point is L = mvr sinθ where θ is the angle between r and v
Conservation of angular momentum explains why planets move faster when closer to the Sun (Kepler's second law)
Angular momentum is the rotational analogue of linear momentum

aha_moment.what_to_show: An ice skater with arms extended (large I, slow ω) pulls arms in (small I, fast ω). L = Iω bar stays constant throughout. Angular velocity dial spins faster as I decreases. The product Iω is visually constant.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: I bar shrinking while ω dial speeds up — their product L stays fixed on a constant bar
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students think something must provide energy/force to spin faster — seeing L = constant makes the speed change feel inevitable, not mysterious

simulation_states.label: Angular momentum defined
simulation_states.description: A rotating disc with L = Iω shown. Torque arrow τ applied → L changes. When τ = 0, L stays constant.
simulation_states.emphasis: L = Iω, and τ = dL/dt — no torque means L is conserved

simulation_states.label: Changing I at constant L
simulation_states.description: A figure skater with adjustable arm position. Arms out: I large, ω small. Arms in: I small, ω large. L bar stays constant.
simulation_states.emphasis: I × ω = constant when no external torque acts

simulation_states.label: Quantitative demo
simulation_states.description: Numerical values shown: I₁ = 4 kg·m², ω₁ = 2 rad/s → L = 8. Arms pulled in: I₂ = 1 kg·m² → ω₂ = 8 rad/s. Four times faster! Energy increases (KE = ½Iω²) — work done by internal forces.
simulation_states.emphasis: I reduced by 4× → ω increases by 4×. Energy increases — the skater does internal work

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts I (arm position slider) and watches ω change. L bar stays constant. KE bar shows energy change. A planet orbit demo shows faster speed at perihelion.
simulation_states.emphasis: Slide arm position — watch spin speed change while L stays fixed

common_misconceptions.belief: The skater speeds up because of some force pushing them
common_misconceptions.correction: No external torque acts. The skater does internal work by pulling arms in against centrifugal tendency. Angular momentum L stays constant, so reducing I forces ω to increase. The extra KE comes from the skater's muscular work.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show L constant bar while ω increases — no external torque arrow present

common_misconceptions.correction: Angular momentum is conserved only when net external torque is zero. If friction or any external torque acts, L changes: τ = dL/dt.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Add friction torque and show L decreasing over time
